q: A small object at rest, absorbs a light pulse of power <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mW</mi></math> and duration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>ns</mi></math>. Assuming speed of light as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The momentum of the object becomes equal to :
question: A small object at rest, absorbs a light pulse of power <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mW</mi></math> and duration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>ns</mi></math>. Assuming speed of light as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The momentum of the object becomes equal to :
solution:
Momentum p=EnergyESpeed of lightc

=Power×timec

=20×10-3W300×10-9s3×108 m s-1

=2×10-17 kg m s-1

As momentum is conserved, therefore momentum of object will be 2×10-17 kg m s-1.

q: The stopping potential for photoelectrons emitted from a surface illuminated by light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6630</mn><mo>Å</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>42</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. If the threshold frequency is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>13</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is (nearest integer): (Given, speed light <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.  Planck's constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>63</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>)
question: The stopping potential for photoelectrons emitted from a surface illuminated by light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6630</mn><mo>Å</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>42</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. If the threshold frequency is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>13</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is (nearest integer): (Given, speed light <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.  Planck's constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>63</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>)
solution:
We know that, KEmax=hf-ϕ=hcλ-ϕ

Or, hcλ-ϕ=eV0, where, ϕ=hνth and V0 is stopping potential.

So, threshold frequency is 

νth=cλ-eV0h

=3×10866330×10-10-1.6×10-19×0.426.63×10-34

=36.630×1015-1.6×0.426.63×1015

=101536.630-1.6×0.426.63=0.4524-0.1013

νth=35.11×1013

q:
The temperature of an ideal gas in three dimensions is 300 K. The corresponding de-Broglie wavelength of the electron approximately at 300 K is:

me=mass of electron =9×10-31 kg, h=Planck constant =6.6×10-34 J s, kB=Boltzmann constant=1.38×10-23 J K-1
question:
The temperature of an ideal gas in three dimensions is 300 K. The corresponding de-Broglie wavelength of the electron approximately at 300 K is:

me=mass of electron =9×10-31 kg, h=Planck constant =6.6×10-34 J s, kB=Boltzmann constant=1.38×10-23 J K-1
solution:
k=1.38×10-23 J K-1

Mass of electron =9×10-31 kg

⇒λD=h2m×32×kT=h3mkT

⇒λD=6.6×10-343×9×10-31×1.38×10-23×300=6.6×10-34105.72×10-27=62 Aο

Thus x=62 Aο

subject: Physics
q: The de Broglie wavelength of an oxygen molecule at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $x \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~m}$. The value of $x$ is (take Planck's constant $=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}. \mathrm{s}$, Boltzmann constant $=1.38 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}$, mass of oxygen molecule $=5.31 \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{~kg}$)
question: The de Broglie wavelength of an oxygen molecule at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $x \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~m}$. The value of $x$ is (take Planck's constant $=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}. \mathrm{s}$, Boltzmann constant $=1.38 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}$, mass of oxygen molecule $=5.31 \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{~kg}$)
solution:
The de Broglie wavelength is $\lambda = \frac{h}{mv}$. For an ideal gas, $v_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{3kT}{m}}$
Therefore: $\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{3mkT}}$
Substituting values: $\lambda = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{3 \times 5.31 \times 10^{-26} \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}}$
$\lambda = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{6.62 \times 10^{-46}}} = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{2.57 \times 10^{-23}}$
$\lambda = 2.58 \times 10^{-11}$ m $= 25.8 \times 10^{-12}$ m, so $x \approx 26$

subject: Physics
q: The de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron in the n=4 level is:
question: The de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron in the n=4 level is:
options: Half of the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron in the ground state
Four times the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron in the ground state
<math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi>t</mi><mi>h</mi></math>  of the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron in the ground state
Two times the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron in the ground state
solution:
De-Broglie wavelength is given as

λ=hp

∵p22m=k⇒p=2mk

⇒λ=h2mk

At ground level energy =E0 so

At n=4 level energy =E0n2=E016

∴ at ground level

λ0=h2m E0

At n = 4 level

λ′=h2mE016=4h2m E0

⇒λ′=4λ0

question:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I : Davisson-Germer experiment establishes the wave nature of electrons.

Statement II : If electrons have wave nature, they can interfere and show diffraction.

In the light of the above statements choose the correct answer from the option given below :
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true.
Both Statement I and Statement II are false.
Statement I is true but Statement II is false.
Statement I is false but Statement II is true.
solution: In Davisson–Germer experiment, the high speed electrons are incident on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ni</mi></math> crystal and they get diffracted just like a wave. So wave nature of electron was experimentally observed in this experiment.

The stopping potential for photoelectric current for this radiation is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><msub><mrow><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi mathvariant="italic">V</mi></mrow><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> If the same surface is illuminated with a radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>λ</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the stopping potential is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The threshold wavelength of this surface for photoelectric effect is___________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A certain metallic surface is illuminated by monochromatic radiation of wavelength $\lambda$. The stopping potential for photoelectric current for this radiation is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><msub><mrow><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi mathvariant="italic">V</mi></mrow><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> If the same surface is illuminated with a radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>λ</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the stopping potential is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The threshold wavelength of this surface for photoelectric effect is___________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
KE=hcλ-ϕhc

e3V0=hcλ0-ϕ  …i

eV0=hc2λ0-ϕ  …ii

Using i and ii,

ϕ=hc4λ0=hcλt

λt=4λ0

question: When radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> is incident on a metallic surface, the stopping potential of ejected photoelectrons is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The threshold wavelength of the metal is:
solution:
VS=hv-ϕ

4.8=hcλ-ϕ  ...(i)

Using above equation (i) -(ii)

3.2=hcλ-hc2λ

3.2=hc2λ   ...(iii) 

λ=hc6.4

Put in equation (ii)

ϕ=1.6

hcλth=1.6

λth=hc1.6

=hc6.4×4=4λ

subject: Physics
q: A proton and an α-particle are accelerated from rest by 2 V and 4 V potentials, respectively. The ratio of their de-Broglie wavelength is :
question: A proton and an α-particle are accelerated from rest by 2 V and 4 V potentials, respectively. The ratio of their de-Broglie wavelength is :
solution:
The formula to calculate the de Broglie wavelength of the proton is given by

λp=h2mpqpV   ...1

The formula to calculate the de Broglie wavelength of the alpha particle is given by

λα=h2mαqαV'   ...2

Divide equation (1) by equation (2) and simplify to obtain the required ratio.

λpλα=mαmp×qαV'qpV   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required ratio.

λpλα=41×2×41×2=4:1

q: An electron with speed $v$ and a photon with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> have the same de-Broglie wavelength. If the kinetic energy and momentum of electron are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> and that of photon are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msub></math> respectively. Which of the following is correct?
question: An electron with speed $v$ and a photon with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> have the same de-Broglie wavelength. If the kinetic energy and momentum of electron are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> and that of photon are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msub></math> respectively. Which of the following is correct?
solution:
We have, λp=hpp and λe=hpe

Given here, λe=λp, then pp=pe

Ratio of their energy is EphEe=hcλPpe22m=2mcpe=2mcmv=2cv

subject: Physics
q: The graph shows variation of stopping potential $V_o$ with the frequency $\nu$ of the incident radiation for three photosensitive metals $X_1$, $X_2$ and $X_3$. Which metal will give out electrons with greater kinetic energy, for the same wavelength of incident radiation?
question: The graph shows variation of stopping potential $V_o$ with the frequency $\nu$ of the incident radiation for three photosensitive metals $X_1$, $X_2$ and $X_3$. Which metal will give out electrons with greater kinetic energy, for the same wavelength of incident radiation?
options: All the metals will give out photo electrons with same kinetic energies.
solution:
From Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons is given by:

$K_{ ext{max}} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \Phi$

where $\lambda$ is the wavelength of incident radiation and $\Phi$ is the work function of the metal.

The work function is related to the threshold frequency $ u_0$ by $\Phi = h u_0$. From the given graph, the x-intercept represents the threshold frequency. We can observe that:

$ u_0(X_1) < u_0(X_2) < u_0(X_3)$

Therefore, the work functions of the metals follow the order:

$\Phi(X_1) < \Phi(X_2) < \Phi(X_3)$

For the same wavelength $\lambda$ of incident radiation, the term $\dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ is constant. Thus, the kinetic energy $K_{ ext{max}}$ will be greatest for the metal with the smallest work function.

Since metal $X_1$ has the minimum work function, it will emit electrons with the greatest kinetic energy.

Answer: $X_1$

subject: Physics
q: Radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></math> is incident on a photocell. The fastest emitted photoelectron has a speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></math>. If the wavelength is changed to <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>, the speed of the fastest emitted photoelectron will be
question: Radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></math> is incident on a photocell. The fastest emitted photoelectron has a speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></math>. If the wavelength is changed to <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>, the speed of the fastest emitted photoelectron will be
solution:
12mv2=hcλ-ϕ ...(i)

12mv'2=43hcλ-ϕ  ...(ii)

From Eqn. (i)

hcλ=12mv2+ϕ

On putting this Equation (ii)

12mv′2=4312mv2+ϕ-ϕ

v'2=43v2+2ϕ3m

⇒v'>43v

subject: Physics
q:
With reference to the observations in photo-electric effect, identify the correct statements from below:

A. The square of maximum velocity of photoelectrons varies linearly with frequency of incident light.

B. The value of saturation current increases on moving the source of light away from the metal surface.

C. The maximum kinetic energy of photo-electrons decreases on decreasing the power of LED (light emitting diode) source of light.

D. The immediate emission of photo-electrons out of metal surface can not be explained by particle nature of light/electromagnetic waves.
E. Existence of threshold wavelength can not be explained by wave nature of light/electromagnetic waves.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
With reference to the observations in photo-electric effect, identify the correct statements from below:

A. The square of maximum velocity of photoelectrons varies linearly with frequency of incident light.

B. The value of saturation current increases on moving the source of light away from the metal surface.

C. The maximum kinetic energy of photo-electrons decreases on decreasing the power of LED (light emitting diode) source of light.

D. The immediate emission of photo-electrons out of metal surface can not be explained by particle nature of light/electromagnetic waves.
E. Existence of threshold wavelength can not be explained by wave nature of light/electromagnetic waves.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
From Einstein's photoelectric equation, 

Maximum kinetic energy, KEmax=12mvmax2=hf-ϕ, where, vmax is maximum velocity of photoelectrons, h is Planck's constant, f is frequency of incident light and ϕ is work function of metal.

From above relation, we can see that the square of maximum velocity of photoelectrons varies linearly with frequency of incident light.

If we increase the intensity of the incident radiation by keeping the frequency constant, then the value of saturation current increases in proportion to the intensity of the incident radiation. Thus, saturation current decreases on moving the source of light away from the metal surface.

The kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is directly proportional to the light frequency, and it remains constant as light amplitude or power of source increases.

Photoelectric effect cannot be explained on the basis of wave nature because experimentally a particular energy called the work function of the metal surface in needed to eject electrons from surface, irrespective of the time for which light is incident. 

Photoelectric effect can be explained by particle nature of light (i.e. quantum theory). Threshold wavelength is maximum wavelength at which emission takes place.

subject: Physics
q: In photoelectric experiment energy of $2.48 \mathrm{eV}$ irradiates a photo sensitive material. The stopping potential was measured to be $0.5 \mathrm{~V}$. Work function of the photo sensitive material is :
question: In photoelectric experiment energy of $2.48 \mathrm{eV}$ irradiates a photo sensitive material. The stopping potential was measured to be $0.5 \mathrm{~V}$. Work function of the photo sensitive material is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{eV} \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}}=\mathrm{h} \nu-\phi \\ & 0.5 \mathrm{~V}=2.48-\phi \\ & \text { work function }(\phi)=2.48 \mathrm{~V}-0.5 \mathrm{~V}=1.98 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

question:

question: In an experiment with photoelectric effect, the stopping potential,
options: increases with increase in the intensity of the incident light

subject: Physics
q: An electron is travelling with a velocity $v$ in free space and when it enters a medium, its velocity is reduced by $20\%$. The de Broglie wavelength of electron in the medium is $\alpha\lambda_0$, where $\lambda_0$ is its de Broglie wavelength in free space. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
question: An electron is travelling with a velocity $v$ in free space and when it enters a medium, its velocity is reduced by $20\%$. The de Broglie wavelength of electron in the medium is $\alpha\lambda_0$, where $\lambda_0$ is its de Broglie wavelength in free space. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
solution:
The de Broglie wavelength of an electron in free space is given by $\lambda_0 = \dfrac{h}{mv}$.

When the electron enters the medium, its velocity is reduced by $20\%$. The new velocity is $v' = v - 0.20v = 0.80v$.

The de Broglie wavelength in the medium is $\lambda' = \dfrac{h}{mv'}$.

Substituting $v' = 0.80v$, we get $\lambda' = \dfrac{h}{m(0.80v)} = \dfrac{1}{0.80} \left(\dfrac{h}{mv}\right)$.

Since \dfrac{1}{0.80} = \dfrac{100}{80} = 1.25$, we have $\lambda' = 1.25\lambda_0$.

Comparing this with $\lambda' = \alpha\lambda_0$, we get $\alpha = 1.25$.

Answer: $1.25$

subject: Physics
q: An <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> particle and a proton are accelerated from rest by a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. After this, their de Broglie wavelengths are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>α</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></math> respectively. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>α</mi></msub></mfrac></math> is :
question: An <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> particle and a proton are accelerated from rest by a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. After this, their de Broglie wavelengths are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>α</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></math> respectively. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>α</mi></msub></mfrac></math> is :
solution:
λ=hp=h2mqV

⇒λpλα=mαqαmpqp=4mp×2emp×e=8=22=22

⇒λpλα=2×1.4=2.8

subject: Physics
q:
An electron of mass $m$ is moving in an electric field $\vec{E} = -2E_0\hat{i}$ ($E_0 =$ constant $> 0$), with an initial velocity $\vec{V} = v_0\hat{i}$ ($v_0 =$ constant $> 0$). If $\lambda_0 = \dfrac{h}{4mv_0}$, its de Broglie wavelength at time $t$ is __________.
($e =$ charge of electron)
question:
An electron of mass $m$ is moving in an electric field $\vec{E} = -2E_0\hat{i}$ ($E_0 =$ constant $> 0$), with an initial velocity $\vec{V} = v_0\hat{i}$ ($v_0 =$ constant $> 0$). If $\lambda_0 = \dfrac{h}{4mv_0}$, its de Broglie wavelength at time $t$ is __________.
($e =$ charge of electron)
solution:
The force experienced by the electron in the electric field is given by $\vec{F} = q\vec{E}$.

Since the charge of an electron is $-e$ and the electric field is $\vec{E} = -2E_0\hat{i}$, the force is:

$\vec{F} = (-e)(-2E_0\hat{i}) = 2eE_0\hat{i}$

The acceleration of the electron is:

$\vec{a} = \dfrac{\vec{F}}{m} = \dfrac{2eE_0}{m}\hat{i}$

Using the first equation of motion, the velocity of the electron at time $t$ is:

$\vec{v}(t) = \vec{V} + \vec{a}t = \left(v_0 + \dfrac{2eE_0}{m}t\right)\hat{i}$

The de Broglie wavelength of the electron at time $t$ is:

$\lambda = \dfrac{h}{m|\vec{v}(t)|} = \dfrac{h}{m\left(v_0 + \dfrac{2eE_0}{m}t\right)}$

We are given that $\lambda_0 = \dfrac{h}{4mv_0}$, which implies $h = 4mv_0\lambda_0$. Substituting this into the expression for $\lambda$:

$\lambda = \dfrac{4mv_0\lambda_0}{m\left(v_0 + \dfrac{2eE_0}{m}t\right)}$

Dividing the numerator and the denominator by $mv_0$, we get:

$\lambda = \dfrac{4\lambda_0}{1 + \dfrac{2E_0 e}{m}\dfrac{t}{v_0}}$

Answer: $\dfrac{4\lambda_0}{\left[1 + \dfrac{2E_0 e}{m}\dfrac{t}{v_0}\right]}$

subject: Physics
q: An electron of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> and a proton of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1836</mn><mo> </mo><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> are moving with the same speed. The ratio of their de Broglie wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>λ</mi><mrow><mi>e</mi><mi>l</mi><mi>e</mi><mi>c</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>r</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>n</mi></mrow></msub><msub><mi>λ</mi><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>r</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>n</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msub></mfrac></math> will be :
question: An electron of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> and a proton of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1836</mn><mo> </mo><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> are moving with the same speed. The ratio of their de Broglie wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>λ</mi><mrow><mi>e</mi><mi>l</mi><mi>e</mi><mi>c</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>r</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>n</mi></mrow></msub><msub><mi>λ</mi><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>r</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>n</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msub></mfrac></math> will be :
solution:
Given mass of electron =me
Mass of proton =mp
∴ given mp=1836 me
From de-Broglie wavelength
λ=hp=hmv
λeλp=mpme
=1836 meme
λeλp=1836

subject: Physics
q: A proton and an electron have the same de Broglie wavelength. If $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ and $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{e}}$ be the kinetic energies of proton and electron respectively, then choose the correct relation :
question: A proton and an electron have the same de Broglie wavelength. If $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ and $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{e}}$ be the kinetic energies of proton and electron respectively, then choose the correct relation :
solution:
De Broglie wavelength of proton \& electron $=\lambda$
$\begin{aligned} & \because \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{p}} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{p}_{\text {proton }}=\mathrm{p}_{\text {electron }} \\ & \because \mathrm{KE}=\frac{\mathrm{p}^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{KE}_{\text {proton }} < \mathrm{KE}_{\text {electron }} \\ & {\left[\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}} < \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}\right]}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The kinetic energy of an electron, α-particle and a proton are given as 4K, 2K and K respectively. The de-Broglie wavelength associated with electron (λe), α-particle (λα) and the proton (λp) are as follows:
question: The kinetic energy of an electron, α-particle and a proton are given as 4K, 2K and K respectively. The de-Broglie wavelength associated with electron (λe), α-particle (λα) and the proton (λp) are as follows:
solution:
The formula to calculate the de Broglie wavelength of a particle is given by

λ=h2mK   ...1

Now, the masses of the given particles are interrelated by the following relations:

me≈mp1837mα=4mp

The de Broglie wavelength for electron is given by

λe=h2me4K= h81837mpK   ...2

The de Broglie wavelength for proton is given by

λp=h2mp2K= h4mpK   ...3

And, the de Broglie wavelength of alpha particle is given by

λα=h2mαK= h24mpK= h8mpK   ...4

From equation (2), (3) and (4), it can be concluded that λe>λp>λα.

subject: Physics
q: When a light of a given wavelength falls on a metallic surface the stopping potential for photoelectrons is 3.2 V. If a second light having wavelength twice of first light is used, the stopping potential drops to 0.7 V. The wavelength of first light is $\_\_\_\_$ m. $\left(\mathrm{h}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}. \mathrm{s}, \mathrm{e}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right)$
question: When a light of a given wavelength falls on a metallic surface the stopping potential for photoelectrons is 3.2 V. If a second light having wavelength twice of first light is used, the stopping potential drops to 0.7 V. The wavelength of first light is $\_\_\_\_$ m. $\left(\mathrm{h}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}. \mathrm{s}, \mathrm{e}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right)$
solution:
Using Einstein's photoelectric equation with stopping potentials:
For first light, $h u_1 = \phi + eV_{s1}$.
For second light with $\lambda_2 = 2\lambda_1$ (so $ u_2 = u_1/2$): $h u_1/2 = \phi + eV_{s2}$.
From given values: $h u_1 - 3.2e = h u_1/2 - 0.7e$ gives $h u_1/2 = 2.5e$.
Thus $\lambda_1 = rac{hc}{5e} = rac{6.63 imes 10^{-34} imes 3 imes 10^8}{5 imes 1.6 imes 10^{-19}} \approx 2.5 imes 10^{-7}$ m.

q: The surface of a metal is illuminated with the light of $400 \mathrm{~nm}$. The kinetic energy of the ejected photoelectrons was found to be $1.68 \mathrm{eV}$. The work function of the metal is $(\mathrm{hc}=1240 \mathrm{eV} \mathrm{nm})$
question: The surface of a metal is illuminated with the light of $400 \mathrm{~nm}$. The kinetic energy of the ejected photoelectrons was found to be $1.68 \mathrm{eV}$. The work function of the metal is $(\mathrm{hc}=1240 \mathrm{eV} \mathrm{nm})$

subject: Physics
q: A light wave described by $E=60\left[\sin \left(3 \times 10^{15}\right) t+\sin \left(12 \times 10^{15}\right) t\right]$ (in SI units) falls on a metal surface of work function 2.8 eV. The maximum kinetic energy of ejected photoelectron is (approximately) $\_\_\_\_$ eV. $\left(h=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}\right.$. s. and $\left.e=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)$
question: A light wave described by $E=60\left[\sin \left(3 \times 10^{15}\right) t+\sin \left(12 \times 10^{15}\right) t\right]$ (in SI units) falls on a metal surface of work function 2.8 eV. The maximum kinetic energy of ejected photoelectron is (approximately) $\_\_\_\_$ eV. $\left(h=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}\right.$. s. and $\left.e=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)$
solution:
Light contains two frequencies: $\omega_1 = 3 \times 10^{15}$ rad/s and $\omega_2 = 12 \times 10^{15}$ rad/s
For maximum KE, use higher frequency $\omega_2$.
Energy: $E_2 = \frac{h\omega_2}{2\pi} = \frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 12 \times 10^{15}}{2\pi}$
$= \frac{79.2 \times 10^{-19}}{6.28} = 12.6 \times 10^{-19}$ J $= 7.9$ eV
$KE_{max} = E_2 - \phi = 7.9 - 2.8 = 5.1$ eV

q: What should be the order of arrangement of de-Broglie wavelength of electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></msub></mfenced></math>, an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>-particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi></msub></mfenced></math> and proton <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></msub></mfenced></math> given that all have the same kinetic energy ?
question: What should be the order of arrangement of de-Broglie wavelength of electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></msub></mfenced></math>, an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>-particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi></msub></mfenced></math> and proton <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></msub></mfenced></math> given that all have the same kinetic energy ?
solution:
λ=hp=h2mE∝1m

mα>mp>me

so λe>λp>λα

q: Given below are two statements : Statement I : Figure shows the variation of stopping potential with frequency $(v)$ for the two photosensitive materials $\mathrm{M}_1$ and $\mathrm{M}_2$. The slope gives value of $\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{e}}$, where $\mathrm{h}$ is Planck's constant, $\mathrm{e}$ is the charge of electron. Statement II : $\mathrm{M}_2$ will emit photoelectrons of greater kinetic energy for the incident radiation having same frequency. In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
question: Given below are two statements : Statement I : Figure shows the variation of stopping potential with frequency $(v)$ for the two photosensitive materials $\mathrm{M}_1$ and $\mathrm{M}_2$. The slope gives value of $\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{e}}$, where $\mathrm{h}$ is Planck's constant, $\mathrm{e}$ is the charge of electron. Statement II : $\mathrm{M}_2$ will emit photoelectrons of greater kinetic energy for the incident radiation having same frequency. In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct and Statement II is incorrect

subject: Physics
q:
The surface of certain metal is first illuminated with light of wavelength λ1=350 nm and then, by a light of wavelength λ2=540 nm. It is found that the maximum speed of the photoelectrons in the two cases differ by a factor of 2. The work function of the metal (in eV) is close to

(Energy of photon =1240λin nm eV )
question:
The surface of certain metal is first illuminated with light of wavelength λ1=350 nm and then, by a light of wavelength λ2=540 nm. It is found that the maximum speed of the photoelectrons in the two cases differ by a factor of 2. The work function of the metal (in eV) is close to

(Energy of photon =1240λin nm eV )
solution:
λ1=350 nm,  λ2=540 nm,  V1V2=2⇒V1=2V and V2=V

hcλ1=ϕ+12m2V2

hcλ2=ϕ+12mV2

hcλ1-4hcλ2=ϕ-4ϕ

hc4540-1340=3ϕ

ϕ=1.8 eV

subject: Physics
q: In a photoelectric effect experiment a light of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> times the threshold frequency is made to fall on the surface of photosensitive material. Now if the frequency is halved and intensity is doubled, the number of photo electrons emitted will be:
question: In a photoelectric effect experiment a light of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> times the threshold frequency is made to fall on the surface of photosensitive material. Now if the frequency is halved and intensity is doubled, the number of photo electrons emitted will be:
solution:
Given, fi=f0. Now, f=fi2=f02.

Since f02<f0 i.e. the incident frequency is less than threshold frequency. Hence, there will be no emission of photoelectrons.

Therefore, current =0 and no photo electrons will be emitted.
